questionPatterns: Tell me about the hardest bug you've had to debug.
Walk me through your process when you can't immediately figure out why something is broken.
evaluationCriteria: describes a specific, technical bug rather than a vague generality
shows a logical debugging process (isolate, hypothesize, test)
reflects on the root cause, not just the fix

concepts: self-awareness about their own role in the conflict
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate on a technical approach.
How do you typically handle conflict in a team setting?
evaluationCriteria: gives a concrete, specific example rather than a hypothetical
shows constructive resolution, not just 'I was right'
reflects some self-awareness about their own contribution to the friction

concepts: what changed afterward to prevent recurrence
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you missed a deadline. What happened?
How do you handle a project falling behind schedule?
evaluationCriteria: takes genuine ownership rather than only blaming external factors
identifies a real, specific cause
describes a concrete change in approach afterward, not just 'I'll try harder'

concepts: specific decision from a real project
what they learned and would apply going forward
questionPatterns: Looking back at one of your projects, what technical decision would you make differently today?
What's something you built that you'd architect differently now?
evaluationCriteria: names a real, specific decision from an actual project (not generic)
shows genuine reflection rather than defensiveness
connects the lesson to how they'd approach similar decisions now

concepts: a concrete prioritization framework or method
questionPatterns: You have three urgent tasks and limited time — how do you decide what to do first?
Describe a time you had to prioritize ruthlessly to meet a deadline.
evaluationCriteria: describes a concrete method (impact vs effort, urgency, dependencies) rather than 'I just work harder'
gives a real example with actual tradeoffs made
shows awareness of what got deprioritized and why that was acceptable

questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you took the lead on something without being asked to.
Describe a project where you drove the direction rather than just executing tasks.
evaluationCriteria: gives a genuine example of initiative, ideally from their freelance or personal project work
describes what leading actually looked like in practice (decisions made, coordination)
states a real outcome, not just intent

concepts: initial reaction vs how they processed it
concrete change made as a result
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you received critical feedback on your code or work. How did you respond?
How do you typically react when someone points out a flaw in your approach?
evaluationCriteria: gives a real instance, not a hypothetical
shows they didn't get purely defensive
describes an actual behavior change resulting from the feedback

concepts: specific technology/skill learned under time pressure
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you had to learn a new technology quickly to finish a project.
How do you approach ramping up on something completely unfamiliar?
evaluationCriteria: names a specific real technology/skill and timeframe
describes an actual learning method (docs, small experiments, existing codebase study)
connects the learning directly to shipping something

concepts: how they sought clarity or made reasonable assumptions
outcome and whether assumptions were validated
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time the requirements for something you built were vague or kept changing.
How do you handle building something when the client or stakeholder isn't sure exactly what they want?
evaluationCriteria: gives a concrete example, ideally from freelance client work where this is common
describes proactively clarifying rather than guessing silently
shows how they validated assumptions before over-investing

concepts: giving feedback: specific, constructive, not just critical
what they look for as a reviewer
questionPatterns: What do you focus on when reviewing someone else's code?
How do you give critical feedback on code without it feeling personal?
evaluationCriteria: names concrete review priorities (correctness, readability, edge cases) not just 'style'
frames feedback approach constructively
shows self-awareness about tone when giving criticism

concepts: specific disagreement with reasoning on both sides
outcome — did they defer, escalate, or convince
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision made above you or by the team.
How do you handle a situation where you're overruled on something you believe is the wrong technical call?
evaluationCriteria: gives a real example with clear reasoning for their position
shows they made a case with evidence, not just opinion
demonstrates ability to disagree-and-commit when appropriate, not just insist

concepts: specific habits or resources, not vague 'I read blogs'
how new knowledge gets applied, not just consumed
balance between learning breadth and depth
questionPatterns: How do you stay current with changes in the technologies you work with?
What's something new you learned recently and how did you apply it?
evaluationCriteria: names specific, real habits or resources
connects learning to actual application in a project, not passive consumption
avoids generic non-answers

concepts: specific technical or product challenge overcome
questionPatterns: Which project are you most proud of, and why?
What's the most technically challenging thing you've built?
evaluationCriteria: clearly articulates their own contribution vs the team's/others'
identifies a genuine technical or design challenge, not just 'it works'
can go deep if probed further on the specific challenge mentioned

concepts: specific instance of a mistaken technical belief or decision
how they discovered they were wrong
questionPatterns: Tell me about a time you were confident about a technical approach and turned out to be wrong.
How do you know when to abandon an approach you've already invested time in?
evaluationCriteria: gives a genuine, specific example, not a deflected non-answer
shows they recognized the signal that they were wrong rather than doubling down indefinitely
describes concretely how they pivoted

concepts: honest acknowledgment of a real skill gap (e.g. Docker, cloud, MLOps)
concrete plan or evidence of learning ability, not just 'I'll figure it out'
transferable experience they can point to
questionPatterns: Your resume shows strong MERN experience but limited exposure to Docker and cloud deployment — how would you approach ramping up on those?
This role needs skills you haven't used in production yet — what makes you confident you can pick them up?
evaluationCriteria: doesn't overclaim experience they don't have
gives a credible, specific learning plan or references genuinely transferable experience
shows self-awareness about the gap rather than deflecting
